questions.question: Explain the DDA algorithm for line drawing and state its disadvantages.
keyPoints: DDA computes points by using slope increment: x increment = 1, y increment = m
For each step, x += 1, y += m and rounds to nearest integer
Disadvantages: Uses floating-point arithmetic (slow), requires rounding operations, accumulation errors

questions.question: Derive the initial decision parameter for Bresenham's line algorithm for slopes between 0 and 1.
keyPoints: For 0<m<1, start at (x0,y0)
Midpoint between candidate pixels is at (x+1, y+0.5)
Equation of line: y = mx + c
Substitute midpoint to get initial p: p = 2*dy - dx
If p<0, choose lower pixel; if p≥0, choose upper pixel

questions.question: Compare DDA and Bresenham's line drawing algorithms with respect to accuracy and efficiency.
keyPoints: Bresenham uses only integer arithmetic; DDA uses floating-point
Bresenham is faster due to no floating-point operations
Bresenham produces exact integer coordinates; DDA requires rounding
Bresenham is more efficient in hardware implementation
Both have O(n) time complexity where n is line length

questions.question: Write the step-by-step procedure for DDA algorithm to draw a line from (x1,y1) to (x2,y2).
keyPoints: Calculate dx = x2-x1, dy = y2-y1
Calculate steps = max(|dx|,|dy|)
Calculate x-increment = dx/steps, y-increment = dy/steps
Initialize x=x1, y=y1
For each step: plot pixel at (round(x),round(y)), x+=x-increment, y+=y-increment

questions.question: What is aliasing? Explain any one anti-aliasing technique used in line drawing.
keyPoints: Aliasing is the jagged/stair-step appearance of lines on raster displays
Anti-aliasing technique: Supersampling or Wu's algorithm
Wu's algorithm assigns varying intensity to pixels based on distance from line
Closer pixels get higher intensity, distant pixels get lower intensity
This creates a smoother, more natural appearance

questions.question: How does Bresenham's algorithm handle the case when slope > 1?
keyPoints: For slope > 1, the algorithm swaps the roles of x and y
Instead of stepping in x, we step in y
Decision parameter is adjusted accordingly
This ensures close pixel spacing and avoids gaps in the line
The algorithm essentially transposes the line

questions.question: Derive and explain Bresenham's line drawing algorithm. Apply it to draw a line from (2,2) to (9,5). Show all steps and pixel coordinates.
keyPoints: Derive decision parameter using midpoint approach: p = 2*dy - dx
Initial p0 = 2*dy - dx where dy=y2-y1, dx=x2-x1
For this example: dx=7, dy=3, p0 = 6-7=-1
Step-by-step iteration showing p values and pixel selections
Update rules: if p<0: p+=2*dy, choose (x+1,y); if p≥0: p+=2*dy-2*dx, choose (x+1,y+1)

questions.question: Explain DDA algorithm in detail. Write the algorithm and analyze its time and space complexity. Also state why it is less preferred compared to Bresenham's algorithm.
keyPoints: DDA uses Digital Differential Analyzer concept
Algorithm steps with slope calculation and incremental stepping
Time complexity: O(n) where n is number of pixels
Space complexity: O(1) - uses only few variables
Less preferred because: uses floating-point (slower), requires rounding, accumulates errors, hardware implementation difficult
Bresenham's uses integer arithmetic only, making it faster and more accurate

questions.question: What is rasterization? Explain with a diagram how line drawing algorithms convert a mathematical line into pixels on a raster display. Discuss the aliasing problem and solutions.
keyPoints: Rasterization converts geometric primitives into pixel representation
Mathematical line: continuous, raster line: discrete pixels
Show pixel grid with line passing through - pixels closest to line are illuminated
Aliasing/jaggies occur due to discrete nature of pixels
Solutions: 1) Supersampling - sample at higher resolution, 2) Wu's algorithm - intensity proportional to closeness, 3) Gaussian filtering
Anti-aliasing produces smoother appearance at cost of more computation

questions.question: Write a complete pseudocode for Bresenham's line algorithm and trace it for drawing a line from (10,20) to (18,24).
keyPoints: Pseudocode with proper initialization of dx, dy, decision parameter
Handling of both positive and negative slopes
Update formulas for decision parameter
Step-by-step trace showing each iteration
